required.title: A quality sketchbook and drawing pencils

suggested.title: [Look Together] Art books featuring strong draftsmanship
suggested.author: Rembrandt, Durer, Degas, Kathe Kollwitz, or Andrew Wyeth. Let the child see what a lifetime of observation practice produces.

competence_markers.description: The child can complete a contour drawing exercise with sustained attention, looking at the object more than the paper
The child can draw a simple still life from observation, showing evidence of looking (specific shapes, relative positions, some light/shadow awareness)
The child has drawn at least three outdoor/landscape scenes, demonstrating awareness that distant things appear smaller and lighter
The child can explain atmospheric perspective in their own words and point out examples in the real world
The child maintains a sketchbook with regular entries that include both drawings and written observations
The child has visited art in person or studied it in high-quality reproduction, and can narrate specific observations about at least two artworks
The child uses drawing as a recording tool — drawing things they see to remember or study them, not only for artistic purposes
The child demonstrates comfort with sustained observation drawing sessions of 20-30 minutes

narration_prompts.prompt: Show me a contour drawing you did this week. Tell me about the experience.
narration_prompts.detail: What were you drawing? What did you notice about the edge of the object that you would not have noticed without drawing it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about your most recent still life drawing.
narration_prompts.detail: What objects did you draw? What was hardest to get right? What did you notice about the light and shadows?
Describe it in your own words. Can you give me an example from something you have seen?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick an entry from your sketchbook and tell me the story behind it.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you see? Why did you decide to draw it? What do you notice about it now that you did not notice then?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a piece of art you looked at in a museum or book.
narration_prompts.detail: What did the artist do that interested you? What do you think they were looking at when they made it?

cross_connections.description: Observation drawing is the foundation for painting and composition work. The child who can draw what they see accurately is ready to paint what they see with color understanding.

Observational drawing has been a primary scientific method for centuries. The child drawing a plant from observation is doing what Darwin and Audubon did — recording the natural world through careful seeing.

Landscape drawing connects directly to geographic observation. Drawing the horizon, noticing how terrain shapes the view, and recording a place through sketching are geographic skills as much as artistic ones.
The ability to sketch what you intend to build is essential to the maker trades. A carpenter who can draw a joint, an architect who can sketch a room — drawing connects directly to building.

This packet builds directly toward Weathering Storms's systematic instruction in value, proportion, perspective construction, and figure drawing. The observation habits and drawing confidence built here are the prerequisites for that technical work.
The sketchbook-as-journal practice parallels the writing journal. Visual recording and written recording both develop the habit of attending to experience and making it permanent.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been learning to look carefully and to draw with confidence. Now you are going to use drawing as a real tool — a tool for recording what you see, understanding how things are shaped, and exploring the visual world with increasing precision.

Scientists, explorers, architects, and artists have used drawing this way for centuries. Before cameras existed, drawing was the only way to record what something looked like. Charles Darwin drew the animals and plants he discovered on his voyage. John James Audubon drew every bird in North America in breathtaking detail. Leonardo da Vinci filled notebooks with drawings of machines, human anatomy, water, clouds, and hundreds of other subjects — because drawing forced him to see things that simply looking would have missed.

You are going to learn contour drawing — a way of drawing that trains your eye to follow the edges of things. You are going to draw still lifes — arrangements of objects that sit still so you can study them. You are going to draw landscapes — the world outside your window, the park, the street, the sky. And you are going to begin learning about perspective — how things look smaller as they get farther away, and how artists use that to create the illusion of depth on a flat page.

Drawing is not about talent. Drawing is about looking. The more carefully you look, the better you draw. And the better you draw, the more carefully you look. It is a cycle that keeps getting better.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Contour drawing skill** — the ability to draw by following edges with the eye while the hand records on paper
2. **Still life drawing** — the practice of carefully drawing arranged objects from observation
3. **Landscape observation** — drawing the outdoor world, including basic understanding of how distance affects appearance
4. **Basic perspective awareness** — an intuitive grasp of how things appear smaller with distance and how parallel lines seem to converge
5. **Drawing as a recording tool** — the habit of using a sketchbook to record observations, not just to create finished art
6. **Exposure to great art** — direct experience with artworks in museums or high-quality reproductions, with sustained looking
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Contour Drawing — Following the Edge

Contour drawing is one of the most important exercises in learning to see. Here is how it works:

Put an object in front of you — your hand, a shoe, a crumpled paper bag. Now, instead of looking at your paper, look at the object. Find one edge — any edge — and start your pencil on the paper. Now move your eye slowly along the edge of the object. As your eye moves, your pencil moves. Your eye leads; your pencil follows. Do not look down at your paper. Just let the pencil trace what your eye sees.

The drawing that results will look strange. Lines will not connect. Proportions will be off. That is completely fine — in fact, it is the point. The purpose of contour drawing is not to make a good picture. The purpose is to train your eye to follow edges instead of filling in symbols. When you do contour drawing, you are seeing the actual shape of things, not the shape your brain has memorized.

Practice contour drawing at least once a week. Over time, you can begin to glance down at your paper occasionally (this is called "modified contour drawing"). But always spend much more time looking at the object than looking at your paper.

### Part 2: Still Life Drawing

A still life is a group of objects arranged for you to draw. Artists have been drawing and painting still lifes for hundreds of years because still lifes do not move, do not complain, and can be as simple or as complex as you need.

**Setting up a still life:** Choose 3-5 objects with different shapes and textures. A round fruit, a tall bottle, a draped cloth, a small box. Arrange them so they overlap slightly — some in front of others. Place a single lamp to one side so the light creates clear shadows.

**Drawing the still life:** Before you start drawing, look at the whole arrangement for a full minute. What is in front? What is behind? Where are the darkest shadows? Where is the brightest light?

Start by lightly sketching the general shapes and positions. Where does each object sit relative to the others? How tall is the bottle compared to the apple? Look and compare before you commit lines to paper.

Then develop the drawing by adding more detail: the curve of the bottle's neck, the stem of the apple, the folds in the cloth. Look constantly. Every mark on your paper should correspond to something you actually see.

### Part 3: Drawing Outside — Landscape Observation

Take your sketchbook outside. Sit down somewhere comfortable and draw what you see.

Landscape drawing is different from still life because the subject is vast. You cannot draw everything. You have to choose: What will I focus on? What will I leave out? This is where a **viewfinder** helps — hold it up, frame a section of the scene, and draw just that.

Notice how things look different based on distance:
- **Near things** are large, detailed, and sharp
- **Far things** are small, less detailed, and lighter in value
- **Colors** become cooler (bluer) and less intense with distance
- **Textures** you can see clearly on a nearby tree trunk disappear on a distant tree

These effects are called **atmospheric perspective**, and artists use them to create the feeling of depth in a drawing or painting. You do not need to understand the science yet — just notice it. Draw what you see, and notice that distant things really do look lighter and less detailed than near things.

### Part 4: Basic Perspective — Things Shrink with Distance

You have already noticed that things look smaller as they get farther away. A road seems to get narrower in the distance. A row of fence posts seems to shrink. Railroad tracks seem to come together far away (even though you know they stay the same distance apart).

This is called **linear perspective**, and it follows a rule: all parallel lines going away from you seem to meet at one point in the distance, on the horizon. That point is called the **vanishing point**.

You do not need to construct perspective drawings with rulers yet. But you should notice perspective everywhere:
- Stand in a hallway and look at how the walls, floor, and ceiling seem to converge
- Look down a street and see how the buildings seem to get smaller
- Look at a row of anything — chairs, trees, streetlights — and notice the size change

When you draw a scene that has depth, let your pencil follow what your eye sees: things really do get smaller in the distance. Trust your eye, not your brain's insistence that all the fence posts are the same size.

### Part 5: Drawing as a Recording Tool

Your sketchbook is not just for "art." It is a recording tool — like a journal, but visual. Scientists, travelers, and naturalists have always kept sketchbooks.

Use yours to record:
- Something interesting you saw today (a bird, a cloud formation, an unusual building)
- A plant or insect you want to remember
- The view from a place you visited
- An object you found and want to study
- The way light fell in a room at a particular time of day

Date every drawing. Write notes next to the drawing: where you were, what you noticed, questions you had. Over time, your sketchbook becomes a record of your seeing — and looking back through it, you will be amazed at how much your observation has improved.

### Part 6: Looking at Great Art

Visit art whenever you can — in museums, galleries, or art books. When you look at a drawing or painting by a master artist, you are looking at what a trained eye saw. Look for:
- **How did the artist show depth?** Light and dark? Size change? Overlapping?
- **What did the artist choose to include?** What did they leave out?
- **Where did the artist want you to look first?** How did they guide your eye?
- **What can you see about how it was made?** Can you see pencil lines? Brushstrokes? Erased areas?

Spend at least one minute with any artwork that interests you. Then narrate what you saw.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Contour Drawing Practice [Weekly]
One contour drawing session per week: the child draws from observation using the contour method (looking at the object, not the paper). Subjects rotate: hands, feet, shoes, natural objects, household items. After several weeks, transition to modified contour (occasional glances at paper). Keep all contour drawings in the sketchbook — the progression shows growth.

### Study 2: Still Life Series [Bi-Weekly]
Every two weeks, set up a new still life and spend 20-30 minutes drawing it. Increase complexity over time: start with 2-3 objects, work toward 5 or more. Introduce a draped cloth. Change the lighting direction. The child should notice how the same objects look different under different light.

### Study 3: Outdoor Sketching [Monthly at Minimum]
At least once a month, take the sketchbook outside and draw from the landscape. A park, a garden, a street scene, a view from a hill. Use the viewfinder to frame the drawing. Notice atmospheric perspective: distant things lighter, nearer things darker and more detailed.

### Study 4: Sketchbook as Journal [Ongoing]
The child records at least two visual observations per week in their sketchbook: things they noticed, objects they found interesting, scenes they wanted to remember. Each entry is dated and annotated. The sketchbook travels with the child whenever possible.

### Study 5: Museum or Gallery Visit [At Least Twice During Packet]
Visit a museum or gallery and practice the slow look: choose one artwork and spend a full two minutes with it. Then the child narrates what they saw — not what the artwork "means" but what they actually observed: colors, shapes, lines, textures, light, and dark.

### Study 6: Perspective Scavenger Hunt [Mid-Packet]
Go for a walk with the sketchbook and find examples of perspective in the real world: a road narrowing, a row of fence posts shrinking, buildings getting smaller in the distance. Sketch at least three examples. Discuss: why do things look this way? Where is the vanishing point?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Contour Drawing Engagement
**Developing well:** The child completes contour drawing exercises with sustained attention. Over weeks, their contour drawings show increasingly specific edge detail. They understand the purpose of the exercise and can explain it.

**Developing slowly:** The child rushes through contour drawing, looking at the paper frequently, or refuses to try because the results "look bad."

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done contour drawing alongside the child? Have you explained that the strange results are expected and desirable? Are sessions too long? (Start with 5 minutes and build to 15.)

### 2. Still Life Quality
**Developing well:** The child's still life drawings show evidence of looking: objects have specific shapes (not generic symbols), relative sizes are roughly accurate, there is some awareness of light and shadow. Drawings become more detailed and spatially organized over weeks.

**Developing slowly:** Drawings are still primarily symbol-based — a generic bottle shape, a circle for an apple — despite the object being right in front of them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child looking at the objects while drawing, or drawing from their mental template and then glancing at the objects to "check"? Try this: have the child draw the still life without looking at the paper at all (pure contour). Then have them do a regular drawing. The contour exercise often breaks the symbol habit.

### 3. Landscape and Perspective Awareness
**Developing well:** The child's outdoor drawings show awareness of depth — distant things drawn smaller, foreground elements larger. They can identify perspective effects in the real world and in their drawings. They use vocabulary like "vanishing point" and "atmospheric perspective" naturally.

**Developing slowly:** Outdoor drawings are flat — everything the same size regardless of distance. The child does not notice size change with distance.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done the perspective scavenger hunt? Standing in a hallway and looking at the convergence of lines is often the moment when perspective "clicks." Point it out, have the child point it out, then draw it.

### 4. Sketchbook Practice
**Developing well:** The sketchbook fills steadily with a mix of observation drawings, contour exercises, landscape sketches, and annotated visual notes. Entries are dated. The child carries the sketchbook voluntarily.

**Developing slowly:** The sketchbook has few entries. It stays at home. Drawing only happens during formal sessions.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the sketchbook accessible and portable? Does the child see you using a notebook or journal? Model the habit. Also: is the child being asked to draw "nice" things in the sketchbook, or is it understood as a working document where rough sketches are welcome?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Skipping Contour Drawing Because It "Doesn't Look Good"
The results of contour drawing are intentionally odd. Some mentors abandon the exercise because the child (or the mentor) finds the results unattractive. Do not skip this. Contour drawing is the fastest route to genuine observation. Keep doing it weekly. The perceptual benefits compound.

### Making Still Life Sessions Too Long
At ages 7-9, 20-30 minutes of sustained observation drawing is substantial. Do not push for hour-long sessions. Short, focused sessions with genuinely careful looking are far more productive than long sessions where attention has wandered.

### Correcting Proportions Directly
If the child draws the bottle too tall relative to the apple, do not say "the bottle is too tall." Instead, teach them to measure: "Hold your pencil up at arm's length. Use your thumb to mark how tall the bottle is. Now, keeping your arm straight, see how many times the apple's height fits into the bottle's height. How many?" This teaches the skill of measuring rather than creating dependence on your correction.

### Treating the Sketchbook as a Portfolio
The sketchbook is a working document, not a gallery. Rough sketches, incomplete drawings, written notes, and "failed" experiments all belong. If the child feels that every page must be beautiful, they will stop using the sketchbook freely. Let it be messy.
